questions.question:
A fruit seller sold a basket of fruits at 12.5% loss. Had he sold it for Rs. 108 more, he would have made a 10% gain. What is the loss in Rupees incurred by the fruit seller?

A. 48
B. 52
C. 60
D. 108
questions.to_find: Loss in Rupees incurred by the fruit seller

understand.plain: The seller sold the fruits below cost at 12.5% loss. A higher selling price of Rs. 108 more would have converted that loss into a 10% gain. The gap of 22.5 percentage points between loss and gain corresponds exactly to Rs. 108, letting us find the cost price and then the actual loss.

keywords.explain: The original price at which the seller acquired the basket of fruits.
keywords.example: If CP = Rs. 480, then 12.5% loss means SP = Rs. 420.

keywords.explain: The actual price at which the goods were sold.
The percentage by which SP is less than CP, calculated as (CP - SP) / CP * 100.

understand.visual_alt: A number line from SP (12.5% loss) to higher SP (10% gain), with Rs. 108 gap marking 22.5% of CP.

given.aim: Find the loss amount in Rupees

terms.connects: Sets up the actual selling price as a fraction of CP
Sets up the counterfactual selling price

terms.meaning: Rs. 108 separates the two selling prices
terms.connects: Bridges the loss scenario and the gain scenario to solve for CP

given.plan: Set CP = x. Write SP at loss = 0.875x and SP at gain = 1.10x. Equate their difference to 108 to find x, then compute loss = 0.125x.

steps.title: Express both selling prices in terms of CP
steps.note: At 12.5% loss SP is 87.5% of CP; at 10% gain SP is 110% of CP.

steps.title: Set up equation from the price difference
steps.note: The 22.5 percentage-point swing between 12.5% loss and 10% gain corresponds to Rs. 108.

steps.title: Calculate the actual loss
steps.note: The answer is option C. Note: official answer key lists D; the computation confirms 60.

questions.formula_note: The profit/loss selling price formula SP = CP * (1 +/- p/100) is used; no existing registry entry covers it, so it is added as a new formula.

questions.question:
A person divided an amount of Rs. 100,000 into two parts and invested in two different schemes. In one he got 10% profit and in the other he got 12% profit. If the profit percentages are interchanged with these investments he would have got Rs. 120 less. Find the ratio between his investments in the two schemes.

A. 9 : 16
B. 11 : 14
C. 37 : 63
D. 47 : 53
questions.to_find: Ratio of the two investment amounts

understand.plain: The total investment is Rs. 1,00,000 split into two parts earning different profit rates. Swapping the rates between the two parts reduces the combined profit by Rs. 120. This small reduction pinpoints exactly how the Rs. 1,00,000 is split.

keywords.explain: Profit as a percentage of the invested amount.
Swapping the profit rates between the two investment parts.
keywords.example: Part 1 originally earns 10%, after swap earns 12%; Part 2 originally earns 12%, after swap earns 10%.

keywords.explain: The change in total profit when rates are swapped.

understand.visual_alt: Two labeled boxes showing Rs. p and Rs. (100000 - p) with arrows indicating the 10% and 12% rates, and a difference of Rs. 120 highlighted.

given.aim: Find p : (100000 - p) where p is the first investment

terms.connects: Contributes 0.10p to original profit and 0.12p to swapped profit
Contributes 0.12*(100000-p) to original and 0.10*(100000-p) to swapped

terms.meaning: Difference between original and interchanged total profits
terms.connects: Creates a single equation with one unknown p

given.plan: Let the first investment be p. Write both total profits, subtract to get the difference equation, solve for p, then compute ratio p : (100000 - p).

steps.title: Write swapped total profit
steps.note: Rates are interchanged: first part now earns 12%, second earns 10%.

steps.title: Equate profit difference to Rs. 120 and solve
Compute the ratio
steps.note: Dividing both parts by 1000 gives the simplified ratio 47 : 53.

questions.formula_note: All steps use the basic profit percentage formula; a new formula entry profit-loss-sp is added to the registry.

questions.question:
The roots of ax^2 + bx + c = 0 are real and positive. alpha and beta are real. Then ax^2 + b|x| + c = 0 has:

A. no roots
B. 2 real roots
C. 3 real roots
D. 4 real roots
questions.to_find: Number of real roots of ax^2 + b|x| + c = 0

understand.plain: When the original quadratic has two positive real roots, both its coefficients a and c are positive while b is negative (since sum = -b/a > 0). Replacing x with |x| in the quadratic means the equation is symmetric about zero, so each positive solution gives a matching negative one, doubling the root count.

keywords.explain: Both roots of ax^2 + bx + c = 0 are greater than zero.
keywords.example: If roots are 2 and 3 then sum = 5 = -b/a (so b < 0 when a > 0) and product = 6 = c/a (so c > 0).

keywords.explain: Replacing x with |x| creates a symmetric equation; a solution y > 0 to ay^2 + by + c = 0 implies both x = y and x = -y are solutions.
keywords.example: |x| = 3 gives x = 3 and x = -3, two solutions from one.

keywords.explain: Sum of roots = -b/a and product of roots = c/a for ax^2 + bx + c = 0.
keywords.example: Real positive roots imply -b/a > 0 and c/a > 0, so sign(b) != sign(a) and sign(c) = sign(a).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 220' width='420' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='390' y2='110' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='210' y1='20' x2='210' y2='200' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='390' y='128' class='mut sub' text-anchor='end'>x</text><text x='218' y='22' class='mut sub'>y</text><circle cx='300' cy='110' r='7' class='ok'/><text x='300' y='100' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>+p</text><circle cx='350' cy='110' r='7' class='ok'/><text x='350' y='100' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>+q</text><circle cx='120' cy='110' r='7' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='100' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>-p</text><circle cx='70' cy='110' r='7' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='100' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>-q</text><text x='210' y='185' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>ax^2+b|x|+c=0 has 4 roots</text><text x='210' y='50' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Original roots p,q > 0  →  |x|=p gives x=±p  ;  |x|=q gives x=±q</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Number line showing four roots: -q, -p on the left and +p, +q on the right, all arising from the two positive roots p and q of the original quadratic.

given.aim: Count real roots of ax^2 + b|x| + c = 0

terms.meaning: Both positive real numbers satisfying ax^2 + bx + c = 0
terms.connects: These become the positive solutions of the absolute-value equation

terms.meaning: For y >= 0, ay^2 + by + c = 0 has the same roots p and q
terms.connects: Each positive root of the substituted equation spawns two real roots in x

terms.meaning: a > 0, b < 0, c > 0 follow from both roots being positive
terms.example: Sum = -b/a > 0 and product = c/a > 0 with a > 0
terms.connects: Ensures the equation ay^2 + by + c = 0 truly has two positive real roots

given.plan: Substitute y = |x| >= 0. The equation ay^2 + by + c = 0 has roots p and q (both positive). Each gives |x| = p (so x = p or x = -p) and |x| = q (so x = q or x = -q), yielding 4 real roots total.

steps.title: Analyse sign conditions from positive roots
steps.apply: Both roots positive => sum > 0 => -b/a > 0; product > 0 => c/a > 0. Taking a > 0: b < 0 and c > 0.
steps.note: These sign conditions are critical: they ensure b|x| behaves like bx for x > 0 and like b(-x) for x < 0.

steps.title: Substitute y = |x| into the equation
steps.apply: For y = |x| >= 0, the equation becomes ay^2 + by + c = 0, which is identical to the original quadratic. Its roots are p and q (both positive), so y = p and y = q are the two valid solutions.
steps.note: Since p, q > 0, both solutions satisfy y >= 0, so no extraneous solutions are introduced.

steps.title: Recover all x from |x| = p and |x| = q
steps.apply: |x| = p => x = p or x = -p (2 roots); |x| = q => x = q or x = -q (2 roots). Total = 4 distinct real roots.
steps.note: All four values are real since p and q are real positive numbers.

solution.result: ax^2 + b|x| + c = 0 has 4 real roots: -q, -p, +p, +q (option D)

questions.formula_note: Vieta's sum/product relations and the definition of absolute value are used alongside the standard quadratic formula to count roots.

questions.question:
In a quadratic function, the value of the product of the roots (alpha, beta) is 4. Find the value of (alpha * beta)^(n-1).

A. n^4
B. 4^(n-1)
C. 2^(n-1)
D. 4^(-1)
questions.to_find: Value of (alpha * beta)^(n-1) given product of roots = 4

understand.plain: When the product of the roots of a quadratic is known, any power of that product is immediately computable by substitution. Here the product equals 4, so raising it to the (n-1) power gives 4^(n-1).

keywords.explain: For ax^2 + bx + c = 0, the product of roots alpha * beta = c/a (Vieta's formula).
(alpha * beta)^k = product^k, a direct substitution once the product is known.
Relations connecting coefficients of a polynomial to sums and products of its roots.
keywords.example: ax^2 + bx + c = 0: sum = -b/a, product = c/a.

terms.meaning: Roots of the given quadratic function
terms.example: Roots could be 1 and 4, or 2 and 2, all giving product 4
terms.connects: Their product is directly given as 4, which is all we need

terms.meaning: Given value of the product of the two roots
terms.connects: Substituted directly into the expression being evaluated

terms.meaning: The power to which the product is raised
terms.connects: Drives the final form of the answer as 4^(n-1)

given.plan: Use Vieta's product formula to identify alpha * beta = 4. Then substitute directly into (alpha * beta)^(n-1) = 4^(n-1).

steps.title: Identify the product of roots using Vieta's formula
steps.note: This is directly stated in the problem; Vieta's formula provides the theoretical grounding.

steps.title: Substitute into the target expression
steps.note: No further simplification is needed; the answer follows by direct substitution.

questions.formula_note: Vieta's product formula alpha * beta = c/a is the key identity; the quadratic-formula entry in the registry covers this concept.

questions.question:
If (x - 1/x)^2 = 2, then the value of x^2 + 1/x^2 is:

A. 2
B. 4
C. 6
D. 8

understand.plain: Expanding (x - 1/x)^2 naturally produces x^2 + 1/x^2 minus a constant, so we can isolate x^2 + 1/x^2 with a single algebraic step. This is a classic identity-based shortcut that avoids solving for x explicitly.

keywords.explain: (a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 expanded gives a link between a^2 + b^2 and (a-b)^2.
Sum of squares of x and its reciprocal, recoverable from (x - 1/x)^2 by adding 2.
x * (1/x) = 1, which is the middle term when expanding the binomial square.
keywords.example: The -2 in (x - 1/x)^2 = x^2 - 2 + 1/x^2 comes from 2 * x * (1/x) = 2.

terms.meaning: The given condition relating x and its reciprocal
terms.connects: Directly contains x^2 + 1/x^2 as a sub-expression
Transforms the given equation into one containing the target expression
Fixes the constant in the expanded form, enabling isolation of x^2 + 1/x^2

given.plan: Expand (x - 1/x)^2 using the identity (a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 with a=x and b=1/x. Recognise that ab = 1. Rearrange to find x^2 + 1/x^2.

steps.title: Expand the given expression
steps.note: Using x * (1/x) = 1, the middle term simplifies to -2.

steps.title: Substitute the given value and isolate the target
steps.note: Move the -2 to the right side to directly obtain the answer.

questions.formula_note: Only the binomial square identity is needed; the quadratic-formula registry entry covers algebraic identities in this context.

questions.question:
Let r be a root of the equation x^2 + 2x + 6 = 0. Then the value of the expression (r + 2)(r + 3)(r + 4)(r + 5) is:

A. 51
B. -51
C. 126
D. -126
questions.to_find: Value of (r + 2)(r + 3)(r + 4)(r + 5) where r is a root of x^2 + 2x + 6 = 0

understand.plain: Since r is a root of x^2 + 2x + 6 = 0, we have r^2 = -2r - 6. This lets us replace every r^2 that appears after multiplying pairs of factors, collapsing the degree-4 product into a linear expression whose value we can then compute by applying the same root condition once more.

keywords.explain: r^2 + 2r + 6 = 0 => r^2 = -2r - 6; any r^2 in subsequent steps can be replaced by -2r - 6.
Pair factors whose constant terms sum to 7: (r+2)(r+5) and (r+3)(r+4). Each product has the same coefficient of r, making the final multiplication simpler.
Replacing r^2 with -2r - 6 reduces a quadratic or higher expression to a linear one using the minimal polynomial of r.

understand.visual_alt: Step diagram: pair (r+2)(r+5) = 5r+4 and (r+3)(r+4) = 5r+6, then multiply to get 25r^2+50r+24 = -126.

terms.meaning: Key substitution rule from the given equation
terms.example: Whenever r^2 appears, replace it with -2r - 6
terms.connects: Eliminates degree-2 and higher terms step by step

terms.meaning: Group (r+2)(r+5) and (r+3)(r+4) so both products share the form r^2 + 7r + k
terms.connects: After substituting r^2, both linear forms share the coefficient 5r, simplifying the final product
The product (5r+4)(5r+6) again contains r^2, which gets reduced a second time

given.plan: Pair factors as (r+2)(r+5) and (r+3)(r+4). Expand each pair. Replace r^2 with (-2r-6) in each. Multiply the resulting linear expressions. Replace r^2 once more with (-2r-6) and simplify to get a numeric answer.

steps.title: Establish the root substitution rule
steps.apply: r is a root of x^2 + 2x + 6 = 0, so r^2 = -2r - 6.
steps.note: This is the core reduction rule used in every subsequent step.

steps.title: Compute (r + 2)(r + 5)
steps.note: The sum of constants 2+5 = 7 and product 2*5 = 10.

steps.title: Compute (r + 3)(r + 4)
steps.note: Same structure as step 2 with different constant: 3*4 = 12.

steps.title: Multiply the two linear results
steps.note: The r terms cancel perfectly: -50r + 50r = 0, leaving -150 + 24 = -126.

questions.formula_note: The quadratic-formula registry entry covers the root-and-coefficient relationships; the key technique here is polynomial reduction via the minimal polynomial r^2 = -2r - 6.

formulas.name: Selling Price from Profit/Loss Percentage
formulas.plain: SP = CP * (1 + p/100) for p% profit; SP = CP * (1 - p/100) for p% loss; Loss = CP - SP; Gain = SP - CP
